elements.sent-2411-001: Reliable deep learning models require not only accurate predictions but also well-calibrated confidence estimates to ensure dependable uncertainty estimation.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is crucial in safety-critical applications like autonomous driving, which depend on rapid and precise semantic segmentation of LiDAR point clouds for real-time 3D scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we introduce a sampling-free approach for estimating well-calibrated confidence values for classification tasks, achieving alignment with true classification accuracy and significantly reducing inference time compared to sampling-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our evaluation using the Adaptive Calibration Error (ACE) metric for LiDAR semantic segmentation shows that our approach maintains well-calibrated confidence values while achieving increased processing speed compared to a sampling baseline.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, reliability diagrams reveal that our method produces underconfidence rather than overconfident predictions, an advantage for safety-critical applications.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our sampling-free approach offers well-calibrated and time-efficient predictions for LiDAR scene semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-007: The confidence value in deep learning quantifies the predicted probability that a model assigns to its chosen output class.
elements.sent-2411-008: Although intended to reflect the model's certainty about its predictions, deep learning models often fail to capture the inherent uncertainty in the outputs, resulting in overconfident predictions \cite{guo2017calibration .}
elements.sent-2411-009: This issue is particularly critical in applications requiring precise model reliability, where poor uncertainty representation can lead to dangerously misleading confidence scores, especially when predictions are incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-010: Proper calibration of these scores is crucial; it ensures that the confidence values truly represent the actual probability of correctness, enabling users to better understand and trust the model’s outputs.
elements.sent-2411-011: Confidence calibration must address both aleatoric uncertainty, which arises from irreducible inherent noise in the data, and epistemic uncertainty, which stems from the uncertainty in the model's parameters and can diminish with additional data \cite{gal2016uncertainty .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Effectively considering the impact of both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties on confidence computation leads to better-calibrated confidence estimates.
elements.sent-2411-013: Bayesian deep learning provides a principled approach for uncertainty modeling by integrating probabilistic methods into deep learning frameworks, rather than using point estimates \cite{kendall2015bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-014: To capture epistemic uncertainty in a neural network, Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) place a prior distribution over the model's weights and compute a posterior distribution over these weights using Bayesian inference \cite{neal2012bayesian, kendall2017uncertainties .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Since exact inference is intractable, methods like deep ensembles \cite{lakshminarayanan2017simple approximate this posterior, estimating epistemic uncertainty by computing the variance of the outputs of different model configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Regarding aleatoric uncertainty, the logit-sampling method \cite{kendall2017uncertainties directly incorporates the estimation of this uncertainty into the training process by modeling logits as Gaussian distributions and sampling from these during training \cite{kendall2017uncertainties .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This approach necessitates sampling during inference as well; increasing the number of samples improves confidence accuracy but also increases inference time.
elements.sent-2411-018: The primary contribution of this work is the development of a novel confidence estimation method that obviates the need for Monte Carlo sampling while still maintaining well-calibrated confidence values, capturing both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties in a semantic segmentation task, as follows.
elements.sent-2411-019: During inference, each input results in one predicted Gaussian distribution per class, each given by its mean and standard deviation.
elements.sent-2411-020: As in the case of standard, point-estimate networks, the class with the largest mean is selected as the winner class.
elements.sent-2411-021: Clearly, if the mean of this winner class is larger than the mean of any competing class by a large margin (where `large' must be understood in relation to the involved standard deviations), we would be highly confident about our decision.
elements.sent-2411-022: More precisely, we define confidence as the probability that our selection of the winner class is correct, given all class distributions.
elements.sent-2411-023: Equivalently, this is the probability that a value drawn from the winner class distribution is larger than any value drawn from any of the other distributions.
elements.sent-2411-024: While details can be found in \cref{sec:Confidence Computation , we note here that this probability can be easily computed in closed-form for the case of two classes, can be approximated via Monte Carlo simulation for more than two classes, and in addition, a closed-form and thus, sampling-free solution for a lower (i.e., safe) bound can be given, which we have found to be quite tight in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore, to account for epistemic uncertainty, we average confidence values across an ensemble of models.
elements.sent-2411-026: This method yields a robust semantic segmentation map for a LiDAR scene with well-calibrated confidence values.
elements.sent-2411-027: In summary, we make two key claims.
elements.sent-2411-028: First, our sampling-free approach generates confidence values that closely approximate true confidence levels, effectively calibrated against the actual classification accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-029: This alignment is evidenced by our performance on the Adaptive Calibration Error (ACE) \cite{nixon2019measuring metric and further observed in the reliability diagram \cite{guo2017calibration .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Additionally, our method produces underconfident rather than overconfident values, making it particularly valuable for safety-critical decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-031: Second, our proposed sampling-free approach reduces significantly inference time compared to sampling methods such as logit-sampling while maintaining calibration accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-032: Previous research on confidence calibration of deep learning models typically falls into two categories.
elements.sent-2411-033: The first involves post-processing techniques that adjust classifier outputs without retraining the model \cite{guo2017calibration, kull2019beyond, zadrozny2002transforming .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Temperature Scaling \cite{guo2017calibration exemplifies this by recalibrating model logits using a single parameter optimized on a validation set.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Extending this approach, Logistic Scaling \cite{guo2017calibration and Dirichlet Scaling \cite{kull2019beyond employ logistic regression transformations and Dirichlet distributions, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Post-processing techniques primarily focus on aligning the predicted probabilities with the actual likelihood of outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-037: However, these methods do not fundamentally alter the underlying model or its uncertainties that contribute to initial miscalibrations.
elements.sent-2411-038: They modify the output to appear more accurate in terms of confidence but do not resolve the root causes of why the model might be uncertain or prone to errors in the first place.
elements.sent-2411-039: Thus, while effective for calibration, they do not address deeper model uncertainties or biases.
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, temperature scaling faces computational inefficiencies due to multiple validation data passes required for parameter optimization.
elements.sent-2411-041: In contrast, the second category incorporates uncertainty directly into the training phase, enhancing models' inherent ability to account for data variability.
elements.sent-2411-042: Methods such as Bayesian neural networks \cite{neal2012bayesian and evidential deep learning \cite{sensoy2018evidential equip models to inherently represent uncertainty, providing a more fundamental solution to confidence calibration challenges.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Several methods for estimating epistemic uncertainty have emerged in recent years, primarily based on Bayesian neural networks \cite{kendall2015bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-044: These methods enable models to approximate the true posterior distribution, improving their ability to capture uncertainty \cite{lee2017training .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Prominent examples include Deep Ensembles \cite{lakshminarayanan2017simple and Monte Carlo Dropout \cite{gal2016dropout , which estimate epistemic uncertainty by computing the variance over multiple classification outputs during inference.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, while deep ensemble models effectively indicate model confidence by averaging softmax outputs from multiple models, they may still require further calibration to align the softmax probabilities with actual outcome probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-047: To accurately calibrate softmax outputs by accounting for the underlying true distribution, the logit-sampling approach proposes a method that assumes a Gaussian distribution for the logits of each class \cite{kendall2017uncertainties .}
elements.sent-2411-048: This method involves directly estimating the parameters of these Gaussian distributions during training, which then allows for a more refined and statistically grounded calibration of the softmax probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-049: During inference, calibrated confidence is computed by averaging the softmax values from Monte Carlo samplings across each distribution.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Semantic segmentation of LiDAR point clouds
elements.sent-2411-050: There are two primary approaches for deep learning-based semantic segmentation of 3D LiDAR data, as described in \cite{guo2020deep : point-wise networks that process 3D input data, such as 3D voxels \cite{huang2016point or unordered point clouds \cite{qi2017pointnet++ , and projection-based methods that convert 3D point clouds into 2D input data using range-view image representations \cite{cortinhal2020salsanext, xu2020squeezesegv3 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Projection-based methods, utilizing the established strengths of 2D convolutional neural networks in image processing tasks, achieve higher accuracy and faster processing speeds compared to point-wise networks, as demonstrated in \cite{cortinhal2020salsanext .}
elements.sent-2411-052: SalsaNext \cite{cortinhal2020salsanext used Monte Carlo Dropout to estimate epistemic uncertainty based on the variance of the multiple outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Although Monte Carlo dropout can incorporate uncertainty in the segmentation model, deep ensembles have shown superior performance for epistemic uncertainty over Monte Carlo Dropout in recent years \cite{mukhoti2023deep .}
elements.sent-2411-054: In our present work, we employ deep ensembles to compute the epistemic uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-055: Additionally, we utilize the logit-sampling approach for modeling Gaussian distributions over the network outputs, enabling the precise computation of our proposed confidence value considering aleatoric uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-056: Our approach builds on the logit-sampling method to predict Gaussian distributions over the outputs of a classification model.
elements.sent-2411-057: Considering the class with the highest mean as the predicted class, we directly compute its well-calibrated confidence value by evaluating the probability that logit scores from this class exceed those of competing classes, thus efficiently capturing aleatoric uncertainty without the need for sampling from Gaussian distributions.
elements.sent-2411-058: Additionally, we employ a deep ensemble strategy to account for the model uncertainty in computing the confidence value.
elements.sent-2411-059: This approach aggregates confidence evaluations from a collection of models, thereby enhancing the robustness and reliability of our confidence predictions.
elements.sent-2411-060: Firstly, we provide a brief introduction in \cref{sec:Logit Sampling Approach regarding the model used for predicting the probabilistic distributions of logits, which encodes aleatoric uncertainty in the outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Secondly, our proposed confidence computation is explained in \cref{sec:Confidence Computation , which is calibrated and based on the outputs of such model.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Consequently, the total uncertainty score for each pixel is derived from the predicted confidence values as $1-confidence$.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Predicting Gaussian distributions over the outputs
elements.sent-2411-063: The idea of directly predicting Gaussian distributions over the outputs of a neural network, in the logit space, was originally introduced by the logit-sampling approach \cite{kendall2017uncertainties .}
elements.sent-2411-064: This approach assumes that outputs in the logit space, before going through a softmax function, are corrupted by Gaussian noise.
elements.sent-2411-065: This logit noise affects the model’s confidence in selecting a certain class as the output label.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this setup, confidence is not represented as a point estimate; instead, a distribution is modeled over the confidence of each class for a given pixel.
elements.sent-2411-067: By sampling from each Gaussian distribution, a range of logit scores are passed to the softmax function to compute the probability vectors.
elements.sent-2411-068: Consequently, the loss function is calculated based on the average of the softmax results from the sampled logits, rather than from a single logit score, as shown in \cref{equ:LS_loss . \begin{equation}\label{equ:LS_loss} L_x = -\sum_i \log \left( \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t} \text{softmax}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{i,t}) \right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{equ:LS_sampling} \boldsymbol{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{i,t}} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i) \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{I})\end{equation} This loss function allows the model to consider $T$ samples from the joint distribution of the scores over $C$ classes.}
elements.sent-2411-069: In \cref{equ:LS_loss , $\hat{\mathbf{x _{i,t}$ denotes the $t$-th logit sample vector for $C$ classes at the $i$-th pixel, which is computed based on the predicted mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu _i$ and variance vector $\boldsymbol{\sigma _i^2$ as described in \cref{equ:LS_sampling .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The proposed method is based on the Gaussian distributions over the logits.
elements.sent-2411-071: As usual, one would predict the class whose predicted mean is maximal.
elements.sent-2411-072: Let there be $C$ classes, for each of which a Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N (\cdot | \mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$, $1\leq i\leq C$ is predicted.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Then, without loss of generality, we may assume $\mu_1 \geq \mu_i$ for $i\geq 2$, so that class $1$ is selected as the predicted class.
elements.sent-2411-074: The confidence is then defined as the probability that this selection is correct, which, given random variables $X_i \sim \mathcal{N (\cdot | \mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$, is $P(X_1 \geq \{X_i\}_{i\geq 2 )$, equivalent to $P(X_1 \geq \max_{i\geq 2 X_i)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: As there is no closed-form solution to compute this probability in the general case, it may be approximated by computing a relative frequency by simulation.
elements.sent-2411-076: Similar to the training phase, $C$ samples $X_i$, $1\leq i\leq C$ are drawn (one from each Gaussian), and it is determined if $X_1$ is largest.
elements.sent-2411-077: From repeating this experiment $N$ times, the relative frequency of cases $X_1 \geq \max_{i\geq 2 X_i$ is computed.}
elements.sent-2411-079: It is easily seen that the required probability is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:McIntegral} P(X_1 \geq \max_{i\geq 2}X_i) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \varphi_1(x)\prod_{i=2}^C \Phi_i(x) dx,\end{equation} where we have used $\varphi_i(x)=\mathcal{N (x | \mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$ for the Gaussian densities and $\Phi_i(x)$ for their associated cumulative distribution functions.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This integral can be approximated via Monte Carlo simulation using \begin{equation}\label{eq:MC-integration} P(X_1 \geq \max_{i\geq 2}X_i) \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^N \prod_{i=2}^C \Phi_i(x_k) ,\end{equation} with $x_k\sim\varphi_1$, requiring only $N$ samples to be drawn (which in fact can be re-used by scaling and shifting a fixed set of samples).
elements.sent-2411-081: For the special case of two classes, a closed-form solution can be given, because $P(X_1\geq X_2) = P(Z\geq 0)$ with $Z:=X_1-X_2$.
elements.sent-2411-082: As $Z\sim\mathcal{N (\cdot | \mu_1-\mu_2, \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2)$ it follows that \begin{equation}\label{eq:confidence12} P(X_1\geq X_2) = \Phi(\mu_1-\mu_2 | 0, \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2) =: \Phi_{1,2},\end{equation} which unfortunately does not extend readily to $C>2$ classes.}
elements.sent-2411-083: However, using pairwise $\Phi_{1,i}$, $i\geq 2$, the following lower bound holds \begin{equation}\label{eq:confidence} P(X_1 \geq \max_{i\geq 2}X_i) \geq \prod_{i=2}^C \Phi_{1,i},\end{equation} which follows from the fact that the integral in \cref{eq:McIntegral is the expected value $\mathbb{E [\prod_{i=2 ^C \Phi_i(X)]$ under the distribution ${X\sim \varphi_1}$, and since all $\Phi_i(x)$ are strictly monotonically increasing functions of $x$, their covariance is non-negative, from which it follows that $\mathbb{E [\Phi_i(X)\Phi_j(X)]\geq \mathbb{E [\Phi_i(X)]\mathbb{E [\Phi_j(X)]$, yielding \cref{eq:confidence .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: To give some intuition, if class $1$ is a clear winner, the confidence will be 1.
elements.sent-2411-085: If the winner class is challenged only by one alternative class, the lower bound \cref{eq:confidence reduces to \cref{eq:confidence12 , and the bound is exact.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: If more than one other class challenges the winner class, the lower bound will underestimate the confidence.
elements.sent-2411-087: Experiments demonstrate that confidence values derived from the lower bound approach closely approximate those obtained through Monte Carlo integration (\cref{eq:MC-integration ), which suggests that the lower bound effectively estimates the true values.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We evaluate our proposed sampling-free confidence computation on LiDAR semantic segmentation using the SalsaNext \cite{cortinhal2020salsanext model applied to real-world 3D LiDAR scenes from the SemanticKITTI dataset \cite{behley2019semantickitti .}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Each 3D scan from SemanticKITTI was converted into a $[64 \times 1024 \times 5]$ spherical range-view image with channels for 3D point coordinates $(x, y, z)$, intensity, and range values, serving as input for segmentation into $20$ object classes commonly found in urban scenes.
elements.sent-2411-090: We modified SalsaNext to predict Gaussian distributions in the outputs to incorporate aleatoric uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-091: We used $150$ samples in the logit space per forward pass during training, with the loss function defined in \cref{equ:LS_loss .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Additionally, to capture epistemic uncertainty, we employed deep ensembles, training five independently initialized models to generate a diverse set of outputs.
elements.sent-2411-093: The classification performance of semantic segmentation models is commonly evaluated using the mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) metric, which accounts for the inherent class imbalance in semantic segmentation datasets and is well-suited for real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-094: To assess the calibration of the confidence values for predicted classes, we employ the ACE \cite{nixon2019measuring alongside a reliability diagram \cite{guo2017calibration .}}
elements.sent-2411-095: In contrast to the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) \cite{naeini2015obtaining , ACE assigns equal weight to each bin in the reliability diagram, defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:ACE} \text{ACE} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} |c_m - \text{Acc}_m|,\end{equation} where \( M \) represents the number of non-empty bins, \( c_m \) is the average confidence within bin \( m \), and \( \text{Acc _m \) is the corresponding average accuracy.}}

elements.sent-2411-169: Reliable scene understanding requires not only accurate predictions but also well-calibrated confidence estimates to ensure calibrated uncertainty estimation, especially in safety-critical domains like autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-170: In this context, semantic segmentation of LiDAR points supports real-time 3D scene understanding, where reliable uncertainty estimates help identify potentially erroneous predictions.

elements.sent-2411-172: In this work, we introduce a sampling-free approach for estimating well-calibrated confidence values by explicitly modeling aleatoric uncertainty in semantic segmentation, achieving alignment with true classification accuracy and reducing inference time compared to sampling-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-173: Evaluated on the real-world SemanticKITTI benchmark, our approach achieves 1.70\% and 1.33\% Adaptive Calibration Error (ACE) in semantic segmentation of LiDAR data using RangeViT and SalsaNext models, and is more than one order of magnitude faster than the comparable baseline.

elements.sent-2411-176: For 3D scene understanding from LiDAR point clouds, this means identifying points where the predicted semantic labels may be unreliable.
elements.sent-2411-177: Ideally, confidence values should align with the true likelihood of correctness; however, modern DNNs often produce overconfident outputs, failing to capture inherent uncertainties in data and model predictions \citep{guo2017calibration, ovadia2019can, wang2021rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-178: Such miscalibration is especially concerning in safety-critical settings, where high-confidence errors can lead to unsafe decisions.
elements.sent-2411-179: Proper calibration is thus essential for building trustworthy autonomous systems.
elements.sent-2411-180: A variety of approaches have been proposed to improve confidence calibration, including post-hoc techniques and uncertainty quantification methods \citep{gawlikowski2023survey, wang2023calibration .}
elements.sent-2411-181: Among post-hoc methods, temperature scaling is widely used due to its simplicity and effectiveness in calibrating DNNs \citep{guo2017calibration .}
